name: Rolipram activated proteasome-linked tau clearance only in early tauopathy mice
summary: A mouse disease-model signal suggests pharmacologic proteasome activation may reduce proteotoxic pathology early, but the same approach did not work in later-stage disease.
statement: In rTg4510 tauopathy mice, rolipram-mediated cAMP-PKA activation reduced tau and improved cognitive readouts at early disease stages, but short-term treatment was ineffective in late-stage mice.
population_or_model: Early-stage and late-stage rTg4510 tauopathy mice, cortical slices, and purified mouse brain proteasomes
time_horizon: Rolipram 0.03 mg/kg twice daily for 21 days in mouse in vivo experiments
quantitative_note: The source reports early-stage benefit and explicitly notes that late-stage rolipram did not reduce tau or improve spatial learning.
caveats: This is a tauopathy disease model, not a normal-aging or LEV-relevant intervention study.
Rolipram and cAMP-PKA signaling have multiple biological effects beyond proteasome activation.
The stage-dependent failure constrains broad disease-reversal claims.
